turns.text: Hello Dahlia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It feels a little strange to be here, honestly. Being back in the Southbridge hospital, walking past the same hallways where I had my first—it’s surreal. With my first, I think I was just blissfully ignorant; I went with the flow because I didn’t know what I didn’t know. This time? It’s completely different. I’m twenty-eight, chasing a toddler around the house while my body is doing all this heavy lifting, and my brain just won’t shut off.

I’ve spent the last nine months basically living in a state of hyper-vigilance. I’m usually the one digging through the research, obsessing over vaccines and screenings, driving my midwife crazy with questions. I know I’m "low risk," and my husband keeps trying to get me to just breathe and relax, but it’s hard. I find myself staring at the heart rate monitors, just waiting to hear that steady rhythm, like I need the machine to confirm what I’m feeling. I just want this part to be over so I can stop analyzing every little twitch and symptom and finally just be a mom to both of them. It’s a lot, you know? Balancing that fear for the baby's safety with the exhaustion of just existing in this final stretch. I’m ready to stop being the researcher and just be present.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

My husband, Mark, has been my rock, but it’s a complicated kind of support. He’s wonderful, truly—he’s the one physically managing our toddler and trying to keep the household from imploding—but sometimes his way of "supporting" me is just telling me to calm down. I know he’s trying to protect my peace, but it often makes me feel like I’m being unreasonable for wanting to double-check a screening protocol or ask about a specific induction preference. When he tells me to "just relax," I feel a little more isolated in my anxiety, even though he’s standing right there.

My midwife, Sarah, is the one who really balances me out. We have this delicate dance—she knows I have a health background, so she doesn’t treat me like I’m clueless, but she also has to be the one to talk me off the ledge when I start spiraling over "what-ifs." I wish I could just let go of the research and trust the process, but I can’t. Her patience is the only thing that keeps me from feeling like a total nuisance. I’m grateful for them both, but there’s a part of this process that feels lonely because the weight of the *responsibility*—the health of the baby, the safety of the delivery—feels like it’s resting entirely on my shoulders.
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

Sarah, my midwife, is definitely the one who stands out. It’s funny, because looking back, I realize how much I probably exhausted her with my constant questions. There were moments early on when I’d ask for the third time about the exact sequence of the newborn screenings, and I could see her pause, just for a second. But she never once made me feel like an inconvenience. She understood that my questions weren't about doubting her expertise; they were about me trying to manage my own anxiety. 

She has this calm, steady way about her that really grounded me, especially when the panic about things like congenital rubella or fetal movement patterns would flare up. She didn’t just brush my concerns off with a "don’t worry, you’re low risk." She actually walked me through the clinical reasoning, which is exactly what my brain needed to settle down. She treated me like a partner in my care, not just a patient on a chart. I think that made all the difference. When you’re staring at a monitor, feeling like you’re standing on a precipice, having a provider who acknowledges your need for information—without making you feel like a "difficult" patient—is everything. It helped me feel like I wasn't just a number in the system.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

Mark’s role is so tricky to pin down. In many ways, he’s my anchor. When I’m spiraling over a medical statistic or obsessing over a symptom I read about online, he’s the one who brings me back to reality, reminding me that we’ve made it this far and that the baby is healthy. He’s the one who handles the chaos of our toddler at home, making sure I can get a few hours of sleep, and that’s a massive act of love.

But I think he finds it incredibly challenging to watch me be so anxious. It hurts him, I think, to see me in this state of constant "research mode" rather than just enjoying the pregnancy. When he tells me to relax, he’s coming from a place of wanting to see me happy, but it can feel like he's dismissing the genuine, physical fear I carry. It’s hard for him to understand why I need to know the *exact* hospital protocol for a newborn screening when, to him, it’s just another box to check. 

He wants me to be the person I was before the first baby—carefree and confident—and I think he finds it hard to accept that this experience has changed how I view safety. He’s supporting me, but we’re navigating this from two very different places.
How did the clinical team engage with your partner or support person — did they feel included as part of the care, or more like a bystander?

The clinical team, and Sarah specifically, was really good about including Mark, even when I was the one doing all the talking. I noticed early on that Sarah would intentionally turn to him during our appointments to ask how he was doing or to make sure he was caught up on the plan. It was subtle, but it made him feel like an active participant rather than just a guy sitting in a plastic chair in the corner of the exam room.

That said, there were definitely moments where I felt like the "lead researcher" and he was just the "assistant." Because I’m the one doing the reading and asking the technical questions, the providers often defaulted to me for every decision. I think that made Mark feel a bit like a bystander at times, even if that wasn't the team's intention. He’s not a clinician, so he doesn’t always know what to ask, but I think he wanted to feel like he was part of the decision-making loop. When they included him—like when they showed him the heartbeat on the monitor—it gave him a sense of purpose. It shifted his role from being the person who just has to watch me worry to being the person who gets to experience the reality of the baby alongside me. It made us feel more like a team.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

It wasn’t a life-or-death crisis, but there was a moment during transition where things shifted quickly. The fetal heart rate monitor started showing a deceleration that really caught my eye. My heart stopped. In that split second, all my "research" turned into pure, cold fear. I immediately started asking about cord compression, oxygen levels—everything I’d read about in those late-night sessions. 

The nurses and Sarah moved in so fast. It was like they flipped a switch. They were firm, professional, and very direct. They told me exactly what to do—change positions, take deep breaths, just focus on them. In that moment, I realized my "researcher" brain was useless. I couldn't be the one driving the ship anymore. Sarah looked me right in the eye, very calm, and told me, "Dahlia, look at me. You don't need to study this. Trust me. We’ve got this."

It was the most terrifying and relieving moment of the whole birth. I had to let go of the control I’d been clinging to for months. I didn't make the decision; I had to surrender it. I just squeezed Mark’s hand and let them guide me. I realized then that I wasn't the expert; I was just the mother, and for the first time, that was enough.
Who else was involved — your partner, a doula, a consultant — in advocating for you or influencing what happened?

Looking back, the only real advocate I had in that delivery room was me, but it was filtered through a very narrow, analytical lens. I didn’t have a doula—partly because I thought I could handle the "management" of the birth myself, which sounds ridiculous now that I say it out loud. 

Mark tried, but he was so focused on keeping me calm that he wasn't really challenging the staff. He was more of an emotional stabilizer than a medical advocate. Sarah, my midwife, was the one who acted as the bridge. When I was in the thick of it, she was the one who would anticipate my questions before I could even frame them, which felt like a form of advocacy in itself. By answering my "why" questions before they became "why *aren't* you doing this?" questions, she kept the atmosphere collaborative.

If I’m being honest, I wish I’d had someone—maybe a doula—who could have taken the burden of "staying informed" off my plate. I spent so much energy trying to maintain a seat at the decision-making table that I think I missed out on just *being* in the moment. I felt like if I stopped advocating or researching, I was failing to protect the baby. I didn’t realize until later that my constant need to be involved was actually a barrier to my own peace.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

The room felt like it was shrinking the moment the monitoring started. It’s this clinical, sterile space—all bright lights, humming machines, and that very specific, sharp scent of hospital disinfectant that seems to cling to everything. When everything was calm, the space felt manageable, but once the monitors started beeping and the pace picked up, the room felt like it was closing in on me.

The equipment was my biggest conflict. I felt this intense, magnetic pull to the monitors; I needed the data they provided to feel safe, yet the constant sound of that heartbeat felt like a countdown clock. It made it hard to connect with Mark or Sarah because the machine became the third person in the room. Every time the tone changed, I’d stop breathing, and that disconnect between me and the people who were actually there to help became so obvious.

Sarah tried to soften the space by keeping the lights low and talking in a low, steady voice, trying to drown out the "hospital" of it all. It was an interesting contrast—the high-tech, rigid environment against her human, gentle presence. I realized that my environment was a battleground between my need for data and my need for human connection. I wanted to be comforted, but I was so busy monitoring the machinery that I struggled to actually let the people around me in.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

I had a birth plan that was, predictably, a bit of a manifesto. It was typed up, color-coded, and printed out for anyone who would take it. I’d walked through every scenario with Sarah, and I’d gone over the "highlights" with Mark until he could probably recite them in his sleep. I wanted minimal interventions, specific delayed cord clamping, and very clear protocols on how the baby was handled right after birth.

On the day, it was a mixed bag. Sarah was incredible—she knew the plan because she’d listened to me obsess over it for months, and she honored it whenever it was clinically safe to do so. She’d say, "Dahlia, remember what we talked about?" which was her way of keeping me on track without dismissing me. 

But when the pressure mounted, the plan felt like it just evaporated. Once the heart rate started dipping, the "preferences" felt like luxuries we couldn't afford. It’s strange; I realized that my plan was a tool for control, but when the moment arrived, it didn’t offer any real safety. The staff respected it until they couldn't, and honestly? In that moment, I didn't care about the plan anymore. I just wanted them to keep the baby safe. I realized the plan was for a perfect birth, but birth isn't perfect. It’s just... intense.
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

The weeks after the birth felt like a complete blur of exhaustion and recovery. I was surprised by how much I truly leaned on my mom. We’ve had a complicated relationship over the years, but she just showed up without fanfare. She didn’t try to give advice or critique my parenting; she just did the laundry, brought food, and watched the toddler so I could finally sleep. It was the quietest, most effective support I’ve ever had.

I was honestly a little surprised by some of my friends. I think because I’m so often the one "holding it all together"—the one with the research and the plan—some people assumed I had it handled. They’d send a text saying, "You're such a pro at this," or "You're a rockstar," which was meant to be a compliment, but it felt isolating. It made me feel like I wasn't allowed to be overwhelmed or needy. 

I think the biggest realization was that the people who were most helpful were the ones who treated me like a person recovering from a medical event, not just a "mom of two." My neighbor, who barely knows me well, dropped off dinner and just said, "I know it’s hard." That simple validation meant more than all the "you're doing great" messages combined. It was a reality check on who actually sees me versus who just sees the role I'm playing.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding has been... heavy. It’s another one of those things where I felt this immense pressure to get it "right," especially knowing the health benefits and wanting to give her the best start. My mother-in-law was very vocal about her own experiences, constantly suggesting I wasn't producing enough or that the baby wasn't "settled" because of my supply. It felt like every time I sat down to nurse, I was under a microscope. Her advice was so conflicting and frankly, loud, that it made me doubt my own body’s capability—which, considering my nine months of obsessing, was already pretty fragile.

Sarah, my midwife, was my lifeline here. She was the one who steered me toward a lactation consultant who didn't push an agenda, just looked at the mechanics of it. I remember sitting in her office, feeling like a failure because I was exhausted and the baby was fussy, and Sarah just looked at me and said, "Dahlia, fed is the goal. How are *you* doing?" That wasn't just about the breast versus the bottle; it was about acknowledging my mental state.

I ended up doing a mix, and while I worried about the "optimal" choice, it was the best decision for my sanity. My relationships with the people around me—especially the ones who felt comfortable commenting on my breasts and my supply—really made me pull back and become very protective of my space. I had to learn to shut out the noise so I could actually hear what my baby needed, rather than what everyone else thought I should be doing.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

That’s a hard question, because I think most people were so focused on the baby that they completely missed the person behind the mothering. People love to coo over the newborn—"Is she sleeping well? Is she gaining weight?"—and it’s easy to get swept up in that, but nobody really stopped to look at *me* and ask if I was actually functioning. 

My husband, Mark, tried, but he was so deep in the trenches of work and managing the household that he missed the signs that I was slipping back into that hyper-vigilant anxiety. He’d see me cleaning or organizing and think, "Oh, Dahlia’s doing great, she’s back to herself." He didn't realize that my cleaning was actually a symptom—if I could control the environment, I could convince myself I was controlling the outcome. 

The person who actually noticed was my sister. She came over one afternoon about three weeks postpartum, and instead of asking about the baby or the breastfeeding struggles, she just looked at me and asked, "When was the last time you cried, and why?" It caught me so off guard that I just sobbed. She didn't try to fix me or offer a "it gets better" speech. She just sat there and held the space for the fact that I wasn't doing okay. That moment of being *seen*—not as a mother or a researcher, but as someone struggling—was the first time I felt like I could actually start breathing again.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

Before the baby, Mark and I were equals—partners in every sense of the word. We navigated our careers, our home, and our life together with a certain rhythm. But when the baby arrived, the shift felt like a tectonic plate moving beneath us. We became these two exhausted, over-stretched, and often misaligned people trying to keep our heads above water.

It’s like we speak different languages now. My language is centered on safety, protocols, and the constant, silent work of monitoring for threats—the kind of stuff I’ve spent months researching. His language is survival and trying to keep the logistical side of our family moving forward. We lost that "us" because we're constantly in "management" mode. There’s a specific frustration there, too. I think he misses the woman who wasn't constantly analyzing every little thing, and I miss the partner who isn't just a co-manager of this household.

We’ve had to learn how to be gentle with each other again, but it’s hard. We’re so busy being "parents" that we’ve almost forgotten how to be ourselves. I find myself longing for a moment where we aren't talking about the baby’s schedule or the pediatrician's next visit. We’re still a team, but the intimacy has taken a back seat to the sheer, relentless work of keeping everyone healthy. I hope we get back to us, but for now, we’re just two people trying not to sink.
How did that affect your sense of who you were — as a partner, as an individual, as a new parent — in those early weeks and months?

I feel like I disappeared into the "maternal" role. Before the second baby arrived, I was Dahlia—the woman who cared about data, who kept a clean house, who had a clear, analytical mind. But after the birth, I felt like a vessel for everyone else’s needs. Between the nursing, the sleepless nights, and the constant, buzzing anxiety that maybe I’d missed a clinical detail, the "individual" me just kind of evaporated.

As a partner, I felt like I was failing at intimacy because I was too busy calculating the next risk. It’s hard to be a lover or even a companion when your brain is running a diagnostic scan on your infant 24/7. That was the most painful part—feeling like my husband was looking at me and seeing a mother, sure, but not necessarily seeing *me*.

I had this intense fear that if I stopped "researching" or being the vigilant mother, everything would fall apart. It’s a heavy, lonely place to be. I didn't feel like a "new parent" in the way the books describe it, with all that blissful bonding. I felt like a high-stakes manager of a very fragile project. I’m only now starting to realize that I’m allowed to put the clipboard down and just be a person again. It’s a slow, messy process of finding my own voice beneath all the worry.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

It felt like a hard pivot, honestly. My relationship with Sarah, my midwife, was the only thing that felt continuous. She knew the history, she knew how much I’d been agonizing over every detail, and she understood the "researcher" version of me. When she checked in postpartum, she didn't just ask about the baby’s weight; she asked me how my mind was holding up. That continuity was everything because I didn't have to explain my anxieties or my clinical background all over again. She already knew the baseline.

On the other hand, interacting with the GP and the health visitors felt like walking into a cold room and having to prove myself at every turn. It was like hitting a "reset" button on my identity. I had to re-explain my background and my concerns, and often, I was met with that generic, "Everything’s normal, you’re just a worried mum" kind of response. It was frustrating because they didn’t see the trajectory; they just saw a tired woman in the office for a ten-minute slot. 

It made me feel like I had to be "on" again—defending my questions, justifying my need for specific information—instead of just being a patient in need of support. It’s draining to have to keep re-establishing your level of knowledge and your emotional state every time you walk into a new office. It really highlighted how fragmented the whole system is once the "birthing" part is over.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

Returning to work feels like stepping into another, completely different pressure cooker. I’m fortunate to have a job where I’m expected to be sharp and analytical, but after months of being in "maternal, protective, hyper-vigilant" mode, the transition has been jarring. My manager is a parent too, and she’s been understanding about the logistical chaos of childcare, but there’s a subtle, unspoken expectation that I’ll be exactly the same "Dahlia" I was before—the one who could juggle complex datasets without blinking.

But that Dahlia doesn’t fully exist anymore. I find myself sitting at my desk, physically present, but my mind is still tracking fetal development charts or worrying about the baby’s latest milestone. It’s hard to reconcile the researcher who cares about professional outcomes with the mother who feels like she’s leaving a piece of her soul at home every morning.

My husband tries to be the "equalizer," but the mental load of ensuring the transition goes smoothly falls largely on me. I feel like I’m constantly pivoting—being the professional one minute and the anxious mother the next. I’ve realized that being a "working parent" isn't just about balancing a schedule; it’s about managing two completely separate, high-stakes lives. I’m still figuring out how to be both, without feeling like I’m just barely hanging on by a thread in both worlds.
How did that affect your sense of who you are — did going back to work feel like reclaiming something, or losing something, or both?

It’s the strangest, most uncomfortable tug-of-war. Returning to work feels like reclaiming a part of my brain that had been dormant for months—the part that enjoys logic, structure, and solving problems that aren’t about heartbeat patterns or feeding schedules. There’s a thrill in being "Dahlia the professional" again, someone whose value isn't just measured in ounces or sleep cycles. It feels like I’m finally stepping out of the fog of maternity-induced hyper-vigilance, and for a few hours, I feel like myself again.

But then, that relief is immediately shadowed by a sharp, physical pang of guilt. Every time I get deep into a project, I catch myself wondering if I’m "missing" something crucial at home. Am I losing the intimacy I worked so hard to build? I feel like I’m constantly mourning a version of myself: I’m losing the mother who could just *be* there, completely unfettered by deadlines or emails, while simultaneously reclaiming the woman I was before the first pregnancy. 

It’s both. I’m mourning the total, all-encompassing bond I had with my daughter, but I’m also breathing a sigh of relief to be back in a space where I have a sense of agency and expertise that isn't tied to the terrifying, microscopic stakes of motherhood. It’s like being a split-screen version of myself, and I haven't quite figured out how to merge the two without feeling like I’m perpetually failing at one.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

I’ll be honest, my online communities were a bit of a double-edged sword. In those early, sleepless nights, I found myself deep-diving into niche forums for parents who also have a clinical or health background. There’s something incredibly validating about talking to people who understand the urge to track every percentile and who don't dismiss your anxiety as "just being a worried mom." We spoke the same language of data and risk management, and that offered me a sense of intellectual clarity that I just wasn’t getting from my GP or the health visitors.

But those communities also fed my obsession. If I saw a post about a rare complication, my brain would latch onto it, and suddenly my heart was racing again. It was like having a 24-hour peer support group that was both my sanctuary and my trigger. 

In person, I joined a small, informal group of other working moms in Southbridge. That was different. We didn't talk much about clinical protocols or growth charts; we talked about the sheer, messy absurdity of trying to do it all. They offered me something the forums couldn't: human connection, coffee that wasn't lukewarm, and the chance to laugh at how hard it is to be both a professional and a mother. They reminded me that I’m more than my anxiety, and that’s something that the data-heavy forums could never provide.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those communities — a midwife, a friend, an algorithm — and how did that shape which communities you found?

It was almost entirely the algorithm. I fell into that cycle where I’d search for something like "normal toddler activity levels" or "postpartum sleep regression signs," and suddenly my entire feed was saturated with parenting content. It was like the internet knew I was struggling with these tiny, microscopic worries before I even fully admitted it to myself. I wasn't guided by a human; I was guided by data, and that’s a dangerous thing when your brain is already working in "researcher mode."

Sarah, my midwife, actually tried to steer me *away* from those forums. She would gently suggest I close my laptop and just go for a walk, but I think she knew that for me, searching was a compulsion. I felt like if I stopped feeding the algorithm my questions, I’d be left in the dark, and that scared me more than the anxiety of finding a new thing to worry about.

The only "human" intervention came from a friend who dragged me to that in-person mom group. She saw me scrolling through my phone during a playdate, looking for reassurance in a forum, and she just took it from my hand and said, "Dahlia, stop. You’re scaring yourself with things that aren't even happening." She was the bridge between the digital, cold, high-stakes world I was living in and the real, messy, human world I actually needed to inhabit. She saved me from my own search history.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

There was a moment about two months postpartum that I still think about when the pressure gets to be too much. I had an appointment with a new doctor, and I was fully prepared for the usual—the quick, superficial check-in, the standardized questions, the feeling of being just another chart in their system. I had my list of "what-if" questions about developmental milestones written in my notes app, ready to fire off.

But this doctor, instead of rushing to the computer, sat down, looked at me, and asked, "How is *your* head, Dahlia? Not the baby’s, not the household’s. Yours."

I froze. I think I actually teared up because I hadn't been asked that in months. I didn't give her the rehearsed, "Oh, I'm just tired, it’s all fine" line. I told her about the hyper-vigilance, the constant research, the feeling like I was the project manager of my own life. She listened—really listened—without interrupting or trying to pull up a clinical guideline to "solve" it. She just validated that what I was doing was an incredibly exhausting, lonely way to live. 

She didn't try to "fix" me; she just held space for me. She told me it was okay to be a "recovering perfectionist," and that I didn’t have to have all the answers to be a good mother. That was the first time I felt like a human being again, not just a set of symptoms or a frantic mother. I left that office feeling like I’d finally been seen, and it gave me the permission I desperately needed to actually start putting the clipboard down.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what really sticks with me, looking back, is just how much the medical system treats "low risk" as a reason to dismiss the *psychological* complexity of the experience. We spend so much time on the physical markers—the heart rates, the growth charts, the blood pressures—but there’s almost no room for the internal, invisible work of being a parent who is also terrified.

I wish there had been more space for the "researcher" version of me to be understood, rather than just managed. I feel like if someone had checked in on my mental state as often as they checked my fundal height, I might have spent less time in that "high-stakes manager" mode and more time just holding my daughter. 

We need to stop treating maternity care as if it ends the moment the baby is delivered and the stitches are in. The transition into being a mother of two isn’t a task you complete; it’s a permanent shift in how you inhabit the world. I hope that by sharing this, maybe the next person who walks into that hospital feeling like they need a clipboard to survive their own birth gets asked, "How are *you* holding up?" before they even have to ask it themselves. It’s the little human moments, the ones that acknowledge you as a person, that really change everything. Thank you for listening.
